titulo: The Creature That Comes Down the Chimney
resumen: A family returns to an old house in Maine and learns why nobody there leaves milk by the chimney after midnight.
contenido: In Millbrook, Maine, Christmas always arrived with snow up to your knees, closed roads, yellow lights trembling behind windows, and that thick silence that only exists in northern towns when the lake freezes over and the woods seem to hold their breath. But it also came with a rule no one wrote on ornaments or repeated in front of tourists: if you heard bells on the roof after midnight, you didn't look out the window, you didn't open the door, and, no matter what, you didn't leave milk by the chimney.
Because whatever came down when those bells rang wasn't Santa Claus, and if it ever had been, it had forgotten how to bring gifts a long time ago.

My grandfather warned me about it one Christmas Eve, when I was eight years old and his old wooden house creaked under a snowstorm. Outside, Moosehead Lake was a white sheet with no edges, the pines bent under the weight of ice, and the kitchen radio played carols so old they sounded like they belonged to another family. My mother was decorating the tree with gold lights, my father was trying to fix the heat, and my grandfather sat in front of the fireplace with a cup of black coffee in his hands, staring into the flames as if they were showing him something he didn't want to remember.
"If something comes down that chimney tonight," he said, without raising his voice, "don't give it milk."
I laughed, because I thought it was a joke about Santa, one of those strange stories old people tell to scare kids before bedtime, but my grandfather didn't smile. He didn't even blink.
"Milk is for the living," he added, "and that thing isn't alive."

My mother heard him from beside the tree and told him to stop filling my head with nonsense, that storms already scared me enough, but my father said nothing, and that was what truly frightened me, because my father was the kind of man who laughed at everything, overdue bills, blackouts, funerals, even bad luck, yet that night he lowered his eyes and pretended to adjust a log in the fire.

Later, when everyone thought I was asleep, I saw my grandfather nail a board over the mouth of the chimney. On top of it, he placed an old horseshoe, a dry pine branch, and a rusty little bell tied with red thread. I watched him from the stairs, hidden in the shadows, until I couldn't hold back my curiosity.
"What's that for?"
My grandfather jumped as if my voice had come from outside, from the snow, then slowly turned and looked at me with those gray eyes that always seemed tired from knowing too much.
"So it remembers we already paid," he answered.

I didn't understand what he meant, not that night, not the next year, not even when I grew up and left Maine convinced small towns invented monsters because they had no other way to explain their tragedies. For years, I thought my grandfather had simply been a superstitious old man, broken by loneliness, winter, and too many Christmases spent staring at a dead fireplace.
I understood thirty years later, when I came back to Millbrook to sell his house.

He had died in November, sitting in his rocking chair in front of the fireplace, the radio on and an untouched glass of milk on the table. The neighbors said he had gone peacefully, in his sleep, without pain, but when I walked in to clean the living room, I found nail marks in the bricks of the hearth, long, deep, desperate marks, as if something had tried to get in during the night, or as if something had tried to get out.

My wife, Claire, wanted to spend Christmas there one last time. She said it would be nice for Emma, our six-year-old daughter, an old house, real snow, a tree by the fire, hot chocolate, one final family goodbye before selling everything. It sounded like a Christmas movie, the kind where old houses only keep memories and not debts, where no one screams in the middle of the night and nothing waits inside the walls.
I should have said no.
But I said yes, because you always believe scary stories belong to childhood until they come back and knock on the door.
The first sign appeared on the morning of the 24th.

Emma found a present under the tree, even though Claire and I hadn't put any there yet. It was a small box wrapped in dark red paper, almost brown, with a white ribbon stiffened by something that looked like wax. The tag was handwritten in delicate, old-fashioned script.
For Emma.
From someone who has known you since before you were born.

Claire thought it was a prank from some neighbor, maybe Mrs. Wilkins, who lived across the road and still left cookies on people's porches as if Millbrook were still the kind of town where nothing bad could happen. I didn't say anything. I picked up the box, felt a strange cold pass through my fingers, and carried it toward the fire to burn it.
Then something knocked from inside.
Knock, knock, knock.
Emma's eyes widened with a clean, innocent, terrible kind of excitement.
"Daddy, it's alive."

The paper moved slightly, as if something very small were breathing beneath it. I didn't wait for Claire to ask anything. I stepped outside with the box in my hands, walked to the shed, and buried it under the hard snow beside a stack of damp firewood. When I came back inside, Claire looked at me with that expression halfway between concern and patience, like she was deciding whether to ask or let it go.
I didn't tell her my grandfather's story. I didn't want to ruin the night. Or maybe I didn't want to hear it out loud, because some things become real once you say them.

That Christmas Eve, we tried to pretend everything was normal. Claire made cocoa in the kitchen, Emma hung a stocking by the fireplace, and I put on an old Christmas record I found next to my grandfather's turntable. Outside, snow brushed softly against the windows, the tree blinked against walls covered in old photographs, and the house seemed warm, almost kind, but there was something in it that wasn't breathing with us, something still in the corners, something listening.
At 11:58 p.m., the fire in the fireplace went out by itself.

It didn't slowly burn down, and it didn't leave glowing embers behind. It simply went out, as if someone had blown from the other side of the bricks. The living room turned cold all at once, and the steam rising from Claire's cocoa became a pale cloud above the mug.
Then I heard the bells.
Above us, on the roof.
A slow metallic jingling, dragged through the snow.
Cling, cling, cling.
Claire opened her eyes from the couch.
"What was that?"

I pressed a finger to my lips, but it was already too late for silence, because then came the footsteps. They were heavy, wet, too slow to belong to an animal, too large to belong to a man. The ceiling beams creaked. Something walked across the house, stopped right above the chimney, and breathed.
From above, a deep, cheerful voice murmured:
Claire stood up, white as the snow beyond the glass.
"God, someone's on the roof."
The rusty little bell my grandfather had left above the chimney began to move on its own.
Cling, cling, cling.
Then a voice spoke from inside the flue, and it wasn't human, but many voices pressed together, trying to sound kind.
"Is there milk?"
Claire took a step back.
"Who's there?"

I slammed my hand on the table to make her stop talking, but the creature had already heard her. From inside the chimney came a low, satisfied laugh, like it had found a house with the lights on after walking for years through the snow.
"There you are," it said.

The chimney began to fill with ash, but it didn't fall like dust. It fell like black snow, thick and silent, covering the rug, the stockings, and my wife's trembling hands. First came a hand, thin and pale, with fingers far too long, jointed like dry branches, covered in soot and tiny bells sewn into the skin.
Claire screamed.
The hand stopped.
Then the face appeared.

It had a white beard, or at least that was what I thought for the first second, until I realized it wasn't a beard at all, but locks of human hair tied with red thread, hanging from an enormous jaw. The skin of its face was stretched like old leather, and where its eyes should have been were two dark holes filled with Christmas lights.
The thing smiled, and its teeth were small, too small.
Children's teeth.
"Merry Christmas," it whispered, in an ancient accent, learned perhaps from listening to happy families from inside their chimneys.

The board my grandfather had nailed over the hearth split with a dry groan, and the creature fell into the living room with a wet sound. It was tall, taller than any man, though it moved hunched beneath the ceiling, wrapped in an old red suit that didn't seem to be made of fabric, but of stitched-together pieces: Christmas stockings, children's blankets, pajamas, scraps of clothing with names embroidered on them. Against its chest, it carried a dark sack that moved as if something inside were trying to find a way out.
Claire grabbed the fireplace poker with both hands.
The creature turned its head toward her.
"Brave mothers," it said, "never last long."
That was when I ran for the stairs.
Emma.
I had to reach Emma before that thing did.
But from upstairs, I heard my daughter's voice, small and calm, as if she were talking to someone sitting at the foot of her bed.
"Daddy... Santa's in my room."

I took the stairs two at a time, slipping on the frozen wood. The hallway was dark except for a red glow spilling from Emma's room, a deep, pulsing light, as if someone had hidden a sick star inside the walls.
My daughter sat on her bed, hugging her stuffed bunny, and in front of her, on the quilt, was another present.
The same red box I had buried behind the shed.
Now it was open.
Inside was a tooth, small, white, a baby tooth.
Emma stared at it, fascinated.
"It says it's mine," she whispered.
"Don't touch it."
Behind me, the stairs creaked. The creature was climbing slowly, in no hurry, as if it knew the whole house already belonged to it. Every step came with bells.
Cling, cling, cling.

I picked Emma up and ran to my grandfather's room. I remembered he kept an old shotgun in the closet, though he always said guns were no use against old things, they just helped you feel less naked. I found the shotgun wrapped in a blanket, and beside it, on the nightstand, was his journal, open to a page marked with red thread.
My grandfather's shaky handwriting said:
It does not enter through houses, it enters through families.
Every Christmas, it comes for the promised child.
If it hears the child's name three times, it can take them.
If it is given milk, it remembers the way.
If it is given a tooth, it claims blood.
Below that, written harder, almost tearing through the paper, was another line:
We stopped it once, but a Christmas debt never dies.
Emma looked at me with tears in her eyes.
"Daddy, why does it know my name?"
From the hallway, the creature answered:
"Because your grandfather said it before he died."
The bedroom door slammed shut on its own.
Downstairs, Claire screamed. I heard one hit, then another, and then a silence so heavy that for a moment I couldn't move.
The creature tapped the door with one nail.
Knock, knock, knock.
"Emma," it sang. "Emma. Emma."
The wood began to split.
Three times.
It had already called her three times.
The air changed. Emma went stiff in my arms, her eyes still those of a frightened child, but her voice came out calm, obedient, as if someone were speaking from inside her.
"I have to go downstairs, Daddy. My present is waiting."
"No."
"It's calling me."
The door opened.
The creature filled the frame, tall, crooked, covered in ash and bells. It was holding something in its hand.
The fireplace poker.
Covered in blood.
I didn't want to look toward the stairs. I didn't want to imagine Claire lying beside the tree. I didn't want to give that monster the luxury of my desperation. I raised the shotgun and fired.

The blast shook the room. The creature only stumbled back one step. The shot opened its chest, and small things spilled out onto the floor: letters to Santa, baby teeth, hair ribbons, crumpled photographs, yellowed Christmas cards, broken ornaments, names written on school paper.
And a little bell.
The same one my grandfather had hung above the chimney.
The creature looked down at the hole in its chest and laughed.
"Parents always think they can break tradition."
It reached into its own wound and pulled out an old photograph. It threw it at me. It landed at my feet.

In the picture, my grandfather was young, standing beside my father as a child, both of them in front of that same fireplace. My father was crying. My grandfather was holding a cup of milk, and behind them, in the darkness of the chimney, a pale hand could be seen.
That was when I understood the truth, and the truth was worse than the creature.
My grandfather hadn't saved our family.
He had only delayed the payment.

He had given something, maybe someone, so the thing would leave, and now it had come to collect what it was owed. That was why my father had stayed silent that Christmas in my childhood. That was why my grandfather had died with milk on the table. That was why the house hadn't felt empty when we arrived.
The creature reached a hand toward Emma.
"The promised girl."

Emma began to walk. I held onto her, but it was like trying to hold someone inside a dream; her feet moved on their own, drawn toward the bells, toward the dark sack, toward that winter mouth waiting at the end of the hall.
Then I remembered the line from the journal.
It does not enter through houses, it enters through families.
I didn't have to seal the chimney.
I had to break the invitation.

I ran to my grandfather's desk, tore through the drawers, and found a metal box full of old papers: certificates, medical bills, photographs, unsent letters, and at the bottom, an envelope stained with red wax. Inside was a contract written on yellowed paper.
It wasn't signed in ink.
It was signed in blood.
My grandfather's name.
My father's name.
And below them, an empty space.
Mine.
The creature smiled from the doorway.
"All that's missing is your acceptance."
"And if I don't?"
Its head tilted, and the bells sewn into its skin rang softly.
"Then it takes the smallest thing."
I looked at Emma, then at the contract, then at the hallway where the red light pulsed like a sick heart. I took the paper and held it to the candle on the nightstand.
The creature stopped smiling.
"Don't do that."
For the first time, its voice no longer sounded cheerful.
I burned the contract.

The entire house shuddered. Voices began to seep from the walls, children crying, parents begging, my grandfather praying, my father screaming my name from some buried December. The creature bent backward and opened its mouth, but no roar came out.
Christmas carols did.
Thousands of off-key voices singing from the bottom of its throat.
Emma collapsed to the floor, free.
I picked her up and ran.

We made it down the stairs with the creature crawling across the ceiling behind us, driving its long fingers into the wood, dropping bloodied bells as it came. Claire was alive, lying beside the tree with a wound on her forehead and terror in her eyes. I grabbed her as best I could, helped her up, and together we reached the front door.
It wouldn't open.
Outside, standing in the snow, were dozens of figures.
Children.
All of them wearing old Christmas pajamas.
All of them holding red presents.
One of them was my father, not as an adult, but as a boy, with the same face I had seen in the photograph. He looked at me from the porch and spoke with a sadness that did not belong to any child.
"Don't let it take another house."
Behind us, the chimney roared. The fire returned, but it wasn't ordinary fire. It was white, cold, and the creature screamed from the ceiling with a fury that shook the windows:
"Christmas always comes back."
My father as a boy pointed at the tree.
The star.

The Christmas tree star was old iron, rusted and heavy. It had belonged to my great-grandmother, and my grandfather put it on the tree every year even though it never matched anything. I tore it from the top of the tree and felt something carved into its base.
So the light remembers the way.
I threw it into the white fire.
The chimney exploded inward.

Not into the room, but inward, as if the house had inhaled with impossible force. The creature was dragged down the hall, clawing at the floorboards, breaking wood, shrieking with the voices of children and old people. Before it vanished into the chimney, it turned its head toward me, and its face changed.
For one second, I saw my grandfather.
Then my father.
Then something far older than any human face.
"See you next December," it whispered.
And disappeared.

By morning, the snow had covered the footprints, the broken glass, and the bells I swore I had seen scattered across the rug. The police said a gas leak had caused a partial explosion in the chimney. Claire remembered almost nothing, or claimed she didn't, and Emma said she had dreamed about a sad Santa who wanted to take her to a place where all the children sang.
We sold the house in January.
We never went back to Maine.

Now we live in Nevada, where snow almost never touches the ground. We don't have a chimney, we don't leave milk out for Santa, we don't hang stockings on any wall, and every December I sleep with the lights on, though Claire pretends not to notice. For almost a year, I believed that would be enough, that some curses need a door, a tradition, an old house beside a frozen lake.
This morning, December 1st, Emma came downstairs for breakfast with something closed in her hand.
"Daddy," she said, "I found it under my pillow."
She opened her fingers.
It was a rusty little bell, tied with red thread.
Before I could take it from her, it rang by itself.
Cling, cling, cling.
And from somewhere deep inside the house, even though this house has no chimney, someone answered with a low, cheerful, patient laugh.
"Ho... ho... ho."
